entity_types.name: LiteraryWork
entity_types.description: A distinct creative work of literature, such as a book, play, or epic poem.

entity_types.name: Author
entity_types.description: The real-world person who wrote or created a literary work.

entity_types.name: Character
entity_types.description: A fictional person, creature, or entity that participates in the narrative of a literary work.

entity_types.name: Location
entity_types.description: A specific geographical place, building, region, or setting where events in a literary work occur.

entity_types.name: Object
entity_types.description: A specific named or notable object relevant to the plot — Excalibur, the poison, the letter. Not generic items unless plot-significant.

entity_types.name: Event
entity_types.description: A named or clearly bounded occurrence that changes the state of characters or the world — battles, deaths, marriages, arrivals, betrayals. Not routine actions or dialogue.

entity_types.name: TextualComponent
entity_types.description: A structural division or part of a literary work's text, such as a chapter, act, scene, or volume.
examples: the 'To be, or not to be' soliloquy

entity_types.name: Theme
entity_types.description: A recurring abstract concept explicitly explored across scenes — revenge, jealousy, power, mortality, honor. Must be identifiable from the text, not inferred by literary analysis.

relationship_types.name: WROTE
relationship_types.description: Represents the act of an author creating a literary work.

relationship_types.name: APPEARS_IN
relationship_types.description: Links a character to the literary work in which they are featured.

relationship_types.name: SET_IN
relationship_types.description: Indicates a primary geographical or fictional setting of a literary work.
examples: The Count of Monte Cristo -> Château d'If

relationship_types.name: RELATED_TO
relationship_types.description: Describes a significant social, familial, or adversarial relationship between two characters.

relationship_types.name: PARTICIPATES_IN
relationship_types.description: Connects a character to a specific plot event they are involved in.
examples: Edmond Dantès -> Escape from Château d'If

relationship_types.name: PRECEDES
relationship_types.description: Establishes a chronological sequence, where the source event happens before the target event.
examples: Murder of King Duncan -> Murder of Banquo
Gatsby's reunion with Daisy -> Confrontation at the Plaza Hotel
Creature's awakening -> Murder of William Frankenstein

relationship_types.name: IS_PART_OF
relationship_types.description: Links a top-level structural component, like a volume or act, to the complete literary work it belongs to.

relationship_types.name: IS_SUBCOMPONENT_OF
relationship_types.description: Defines a structural hierarchy within a text, where one component is a direct sub-part of another.
examples: Pride and Prejudice, Chapter 1 -> Pride and Prejudice, Volume 1

relationship_types.name: OCCURS_IN
relationship_types.description: Places a narrative event within a specific structural part of the text, like a chapter or scene.
examples: 'To be, or not to be' soliloquy -> Hamlet, Act 3, Scene 1
Mad Hatter's tea party -> Alice's Adventures in Wonderland, Chapter 7

relationship_types.name: MANIFESTS_THEME
relationship_types.description: Connects a specific event in the plot to an abstract theme it represents or explores.
examples: Dantès's actions against his enemies -> Revenge
Victor's creation of the monster -> Scientific Responsibility

relationship_types.name: USES
relationship_types.description: Represents a character possessing or using a significant, named object.

relationship_types.name: TRAVELS_TO
relationship_types.description: Represents a character's movement or journey to a specific location.

relationship_types.name: CAUSES
relationship_types.description: Indicates that one event directly causes or triggers another event.
examples: Lydia's elopement with Wickham -> Darcy's intervention
Murder of William Frankenstein -> Justine's trial and execution
Gatsby's confrontation with Tom -> Myrtle Wilson's death

relationship_types.name: OCCURS_AT
relationship_types.description: Places a narrative event at a specific geographical location where it takes place.
examples: Mad Hatter's tea party -> the garden
